notes.note: Craterus was a celebrated physician of the day, attending Attica in her illness; "I agree with Craterus" means Cicero defers to the doctor's prognosis. The name became almost proverbial for a great physician.

Renders "ille ardor," the searing grief for his daughter Tullia, who had died only weeks before; Cicero insists it presses on him despite his struggle to master it. The image of grief as a fire recurs through the Astura letters.

notes.anchor: excused day by day on grounds of illness
notes.note: Cicero, as an augur, was obliged to attend priestly banquets and other gatherings he now wished to avoid in his mourning; the device is a formal sworn excuse ("excusatio") of illness, renewed daily. Roman practice allowed a sworn plea of sickness ("morbus") to free a man from such public duties.

notes.anchor: no one you ask will refuse to swear it
notes.note: Friends could swear on a man's behalf that he was genuinely too ill to attend; Cicero lists Laenas, Gaius Septimius, and Lucius Statilius as men who will take the oath for him. If that fails he will appear and swear to a "perpetual illness" himself.

The "hiding-place" (latibulum) is the suburban property Cicero is seeking partly as a retreat and partly as the site for Tullia's projected shrine. The grief-driven property hunt dominates the rest of the book.
